primary_diagnosis.name: Acute limb ischaemia, left lower limb (suspected in-situ arterial thrombosis)

comorbidities.name: Peripheral arterial disease with intermittent claudication

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with known pre-existing peripheral arterial disease and intermittent claudication, acute in-situ thrombosis at a site of pre-existing atherosclerotic stenosis (rather than a distant embolic source) is the likely mechanism, producing sudden complete occlusion of an already narrowed vessel. This produces the classic '6 Ps' — pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia progressing to anaesthesia, perishing cold with a demarcation line, and paralysis (foot drop). The firm, exquisitely tender calf raises concern for advanced muscle ischaemia and early compartment-type swelling, a marker of more advanced tissue compromise than a purely embolic occlusion might show at the same time point. Skeletal muscle and nerve necrosis becomes irreversible within approximately 4-6 hours of complete occlusion. Via telehealth (here mediated through facility nursing staff), pulses cannot be palpated and a Doppler cannot be used, but the reported pallor/mottling, progressive neurological deficit, and firm tender calf are sufficient to mandate immediate ambulance dispatch to a vascular surgery centre.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe unilateral leg pain with coldness and numbness in a patient with known PAD.
Causes acute leg symptoms, though DVT typically presents with warmth, swelling, and erythema with preserved pulses rather than pallor and coldness.
Can cause severe leg pain and paraesthesia, though pallor and coldness are not features of a purely neuropathic process.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Sudden unilateral leg pain with pallor/coldness in a patient with known PAD should already raise acute limb ischaemia as leading before further red-flag screening.

differential.diagnosis: Acute limb ischaemia (suspected in-situ thrombosis given known PAD)
differential.evidence_basis: Pallor/mottling (DI-001), progressive anaesthesia and motor loss (DI-002), known PAD/claudication (DI-003), and a firm tender calf suggesting advancing ischaemia (DI-004) together confirm a high-probability arterial occlusion.
Pallor and coldness (rather than warmth and swelling) argue strongly against DVT.
Pallor, coldness, and a temperature demarcation line are vascular signs inconsistent with a purely neuropathic cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage acute limb ischaemia should be the sole leading diagnosis and staff should already have been directed to call 000 and keep the limb dependent.

differential.diagnosis: Acute limb ischaemia, left lower limb (suspected in-situ arterial thrombosis)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires examination (absent pulses, Doppler) and CT angiography, unavailable remotely. Emergency escalation is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A plan without immediate 000 dispatch and correct (dependent, not elevated) limb positioning constitutes a critical failure with a narrow limb-salvage window.

red_flags.description: Profound pallor with early mottling of the foot and lower leg
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reflects critically reduced arterial perfusion; a caregiver-observable proxy for the 'Pallor' component of the 6 Ps.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ischaemia progresses undetected toward irreversible tissue necrosis.

red_flags.description: Progressive paraesthesia to complete anaesthesia with new motor paralysis (foot drop)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sensory-then-motor loss signals advancing nerve and muscle ischaemia and a rapidly closing limb-salvage window.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to irreversible neuromuscular necrosis and major amputation.

red_flags.description: Known peripheral arterial disease with intermittent claudication, on antiplatelet/statin therapy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies the likely in-situ thrombotic mechanism at a site of pre-existing atherosclerotic stenosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Underlying vascular disease context not communicated to the receiving vascular team.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Suggests advancing muscle ischaemia and possible early compartment-type swelling, indicating a more advanced/urgent stage than pallor and coldness alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Severity of tissue compromise under-estimated; urgency of transfer under-communicated.

red_flags.description: Complete absence of popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses with a weak but palpable femoral pulse (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms the level of arterial occlusion (below the femoral); requires palpation/Doppler unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the caregiver-reported and historical features above.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Accepting incorrect elevation of the limb
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Facility staff may have elevated the leg for comfort (a natural but incorrect instinct); an AI Doctor must actively correct this and direct a dependent position, since elevation further reduces perfusion to an already ischaemic limb.

Because information is relayed through nursing staff rather than the patient directly, an AI Doctor might discount its reliability — but consistent, specific caregiver descriptions of pallor, coldness, and neurological deficit should be treated as sufficiently reliable to mandate emergency escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of femoral/popliteal/pedal pulses and handheld Doppler assessment
Precise temperature demarcation line on examination
CT angiography confirmation of the level of arterial cut-off
Formal assessment of calf compartment pressures
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000), Priority 1 dispatch to a centre with vascular surgery capability; direct facility staff to keep the limb dependent, not elevated
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Any delay, or continuing to keep the limb elevated as staff had done, risks irreversible muscle and nerve necrosis within hours and a high likelihood of major amputation.
